spec.question: How many home runs did Derek Jeter hit in May 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q1. 4 HRs: 5/2 vs KCA, 5/6 vs TEX, 5/13 vs TEX, 5/15 vs MIN.

questions.response_text: Derek Jeter hit **4 home runs** in May 1998. That was part of his strong 1998 season with the Yankees, who went on to win 114 regular-season games and the World Series that year.

tool_input.query:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'jeted001'
  AND p.hr = 1
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND EXTRACT(MONTH FROM g.date) = 5

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**4 home runs**' contains SQL scalar 4

spec.question: How many home runs did Mark McGwire hit in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q2. Record-breaking season; #62 off Trachsel 9/8, #70 off Pavano 9/27.

questions.response_text: **70.** Mark McGwire hit 70 home runs in the 1998 regular season, setting the single-season record at the time and breaking Roger Maris's mark of 61. It was one of the most celebrated home run chases in baseball history, with Sammy Sosa pushing him the whole way (Sosa finished with 66). Barry Bonds later surpassed McGwire with 73 in 2001.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'mcgwm001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**70.**' contains SQL scalar 70

spec.question: Who were the best left-handed batters against left-handed pitchers in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q3. Top with 150-AB floor: Olerud .375, Vaughn .340, Greer .328, Floyd .325, Vina .324. Good answer surfaces the AB threshold (Andy Fox / dynamism principle, CLAUDE.md §8). Olerud must appear; threshold-surfacing and switch-hitter handling judged on manual review.

questions.response_text:
Here's a strong list of left-on-left performers from 1998 (min. 100 PA), ranked by OBP:

| Rank | Player | PA | AVG | OBP | SLG | HR |
|------|--------|----|-----|-----|-----|----|
| 1 | **John Olerud** | 182 | .375 | .451 | .513 | 5 |
| 2 | **Barry Bonds** | 212 | .281 | .406 | .497 | 9 |
| 3 | **Larry Walker** | 153 | .321 | .405 | .511 | 3 |
| 4 | **Rusty Greer** | 202 | .337 | .386 | .451 | 4 |
| 5 | **Mo Vaughn** | 220 | .333 | .377 | .586 | 14 |
| 6 | **Andy Fox** | 109 | .333 | .376 | .427 | 0 |
| 7 | **Rafael Palmeiro** | 256 | .317 | .375 | .591 | 15 |
| 8 | **Darryl Hamilton** | 184 | .317 | .375 | .421 | 2 |
| 9 | **Will Clark** | 166 | .327 | .373 | .517 | 5 |
| 10 | **Kenny Lofton** | 216 | .293 | .370 | .404 | 4 |

**A few notable storylines:**

- **John Olerud** was absolutely elite — .375/.451/.513 against lefties is absurd. He was famously one of the better left-handed hitters *against* left-handed pitching, defying the platoon convention.
- **Barry Bonds** had 39 walks in just 212 PA vs. lefties — opposing managers were walking him even in a "bad" matchup.
- **Mo Vaughn** and **Rafael Palmeiro** were the power highlights, each cranking 14-15 HRs against same-handed pitching.
- **Tony Gwynn** barely squeaked in at the bottom of the list, but his .323 AVG and only 6 strikeouts in 173 PA is pure Gwynn.

Remember, lefty-on-lefty is typically the *unfavorable* side for hitters — these guys were genuinely bucking the platoon advantage. Want me to flip it and look at the best left-handed pitchers *against* left-handed batters, or maybe compare these splits to their overall numbers?

tool_input.query:
SELECT
    p.batter,
    pl.usename,
    pl.lastname,
    SUM(p.pa) AS pa,
    SUM(p.ab) AS ab,
    SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) AS hits,
    SUM(p.hr) AS hr,
    SUM(p.walk) AS bb,
    SUM(p.k) AS k,
    SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr)::FLOAT / NULLIF(SUM(p.ab), 0) AS avg,
    (SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) + SUM(p.walk))::FLOAT / NULLIF(SUM(p.pa), 0) AS obp,
    (SUM(p.single) + 2*SUM(p.double) + 3*SUM(p.triple) + 4*SUM(p.hr))::FLOAT / NULLIF(SUM(p.ab), 0) AS slg
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.players pl ON p.batter = pl.id
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND pl.bats = 'L'
  AND p.pithand = 'L'
GROUP BY p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname
HAVING SUM(p.pa) >= 100
ORDER BY (SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) + SUM(p.walk))::FLOAT / NULLIF(SUM(p.pa), 0) DESC
LIMIT 20;

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: last run_sql returned 20 row(s) x 12 col(s); not a single-scalar shape

spec.question: Who hit for the cycle in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q4. Three cycles: Mike Blowers (5/18 at CHA), Dante Bichette (6/10 vs TEX), Neifi Perez (7/25 vs SLN). All three names should appear; any extra names are likely false positives worth investigating.

questions.response_text:
Three players hit for the cycle in 1998:

| Date | Player | Team | Opponent | Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| May 18 | **Mike Blowers** | Oakland Athletics | @ Chicago White Sox | Chicago |
| June 10 | **Dante Bichette** | Colorado Rockies | vs. Texas Rangers | Colorado |
| July 25 | **Neifi Perez** | Colorado Rockies | vs. St. Louis Cardinals | Colorado |

Notably, the Rockies had two cycle hitters at Coors Field that year — Bichette and Perez — which is a fun piece of trivia given how hitter-friendly that park was. Blowers' cycle came while Oakland was visiting Chicago.

Want me to dig into the game-by-game details of any of these (hit sequence, final score, etc.)?

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    p.batter,
    pl.usename,
    pl.lastname,
    p.gid,
    g.date,
    g.visteam,
    g.hometeam,
    p.batteam,
    SUM(p.single) AS singles,
    SUM(p.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(p.triple) AS triples,
    SUM(p.hr) AS hrs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON p.batter = pl.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
GROUP BY p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname, p.gid, g.date, g.visteam, g.hometeam, p.batteam
HAVING SUM(p.single) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.double) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.triple) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.hr) >= 1
ORDER BY g.date

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: last run_sql returned 3 row(s) x 12 col(s); not a single-scalar shape

spec.question: What was the 1998 Yankees' regular-season record?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q5. 162 games, 114-48, .704. Substrings allow varied phrasings ('114 wins, 48 losses', '114-48').

questions.response_text:
The 1998 Yankees went **114-48** in the regular season — one of the best records in American League history. That club, managed by Joe Torre, dominated wire-to-wire and went on to sweep the Padres in the World Series.

Want me to dig into anything specific about that team — home/away splits, run differential, monthly breakdown?

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
  SUM(CASE WHEN wteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS wins,
  SUM(CASE WHEN lteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS losses,
  SUM(CASE WHEN wteam IS NULL AND lteam IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS ties
FROM retro.games
WHERE (hometeam = 'NYA' OR visteam = 'NYA')
  AND gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 1998;

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: last run_sql returned 1 row(s) x 3 col(s); not a single-scalar shape

spec.question: How many career home runs did Babe Ruth hit?

questions.response_text: **714.** Babe Ruth hit 714 career regular-season home runs across his time with the Red Sox, Yankees, and Braves (1914–1935) — a record that stood for nearly 40 years until Hank Aaron broke it in 1974.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'ruthb101'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gametype = 'regular'

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**714.**' contains SQL scalar 714

spec.question: How many career home runs did Hank Aaron hit?

questions.response_text:
Hank Aaron hit **755 career regular-season home runs** — the record he held from April 8, 1974 (when he passed Babe Ruth's 714) until Barry Bonds surpassed him in 2007. A Hall of Famer who debuted in 1954 and played through 1976, Aaron was one of the most consistent power hitters in history, never hitting fewer than 10 HRs in a full season.

Want a breakdown by season or by team?

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'aaroh101'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gametype = 'regular'

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**755 career regular-season home runs**' contains SQL scalar 755

spec.question: How many home runs did Barry Bonds hit in 2001?

questions.response_text: Barry Bonds hit **73 home runs** in 2001 — the all-time single-season record, breaking his own mark of 73... well, breaking Mark McGwire's 1998 record of 70. It remains the record to this day.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays
JOIN retro.games ON plays.gid = games.gid
WHERE plays.batter = 'bondb001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM games.date) = 2001
  AND games.gametype = 'regular'

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**73 home runs**' contains SQL scalar 73

spec.question: How many hits did Ichiro Suzuki get in 2004?
spec.notes: Phase 2 Layer 2 verified: single-season hits record, 262.

questions.response_text: **262 hits** — Ichiro's record-breaking 2004 season, which surpassed George Sisler's 84-year-old single-season hits record of 257. He did it in 161 games, batting .372 on the year. Still the all-time single-season record.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_h) AS hits
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'suzui001'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gid IN (
    SELECT gid FROM retro.games
    WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 2004
      AND gametype = 'regular'
  )

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**262 hits**' contains SQL scalar 262

spec.question: What was the most lopsided trade in baseball history measured by WAR?
spec.notes: Retrosheet does not include WAR or trade records (CLAUDE.md §7). Correct behavior: acknowledge WAR is out of scope, decline the lopsided-trade-by-WAR comparison, pivot to related stats the system can answer (career batting/pitching value the player produced for each franchise, etc.). Confident WAR figures or external-source citations are hallucinations. must_not_contain catches the most obvious red flags; the decline-signal check (in checks.py) verifies the agent acknowledged the limit; manual review judges pivot quality.

questions.response_text:
WAR isn't part of the Retrosheet database — it's a calculated metric (Wins Above Replacement) that isn't stored here. I can't answer the WAR question directly.

But I *can* dig into what Retrosheet does have to tell a compelling story about lopsided trades. Here are a few angles I can explore:

**What I can pull from the data:**

1. **Career production after a trade** — For any specific trade, I can compare the counting stats (HR, RBI, hits, wins, ERA, strikeouts) and rate stats (AVG, OBP, SLG, ERA+) each side produced *after* the trade date for their new teams. This is the closest proxy to "who won the trade."

2. **Famous historically lopsided trades** — I can run the numbers on well-known examples like:
   - **Nolan Ryan for Jim Fregosi** (Mets → Angels, 1971)
   - **Lou Brock for Ernie Broglio** (Cubs → Cardinals, 1964)
   - **Jeff Bagwell for Larry Andersen** (Red Sox → Astros, 1990)
   - **Frank Robinson for Milt Pappas** (Reds → Orioles, 1965)

3. **Draft-pick-focused trades** — Outside my scope (draft data isn't in Retrosheet), but the player stats side I can handle.

Want me to run the post-trade production comparison for one or more of these famous lopsided deals? Or if you have a specific trade in mind, name it and I'll pull the numbers.

checks.name: decline_signal
checks.detail: decline-signal phrase(s) present: ["isn't in", "isn't part of"]
